title: Steady Through Change: In Policy, Business, and Life
summary: Daly frames monetary policy as 'steady through change'—anchor on the dual mandate, assess, and decide repeatedly. She explains that the FOMC held rates through most of 2025 to keep policy 'modestly restrictive' and keep downward pressure on inflation while watching the labor market, then cut 25 basis points at the September meeting (which she fully supported) because growth, spending, and the labor market had slowed and inflation rose less than expected, remaining confined to tariff-affected sectors. She signals that 'further policy adjustments will be needed' to restore price stability while supporting the labor market, but stresses these are projections, not promises. Her stance reads as a dovish-leaning, gradual easing bias still anchored on the 2 percent inflation goal.

key_quotes.quote: So, we left the interest rate unchanged, keeping policy modestly restrictive, continuing to put downward pressure on inflation.
key_quotes.context: Describing the FOMC's stance prior to the September meeting; her read of how restrictive policy is.

key_quotes.quote: That moment came last week. At our most recent FOMC meeting, we lowered the policy rate by 25 basis points. A decision I fully supported.
Moving forward, it is likely that further policy adjustments will be needed as we work to restore price stability while providing needed support to the labor market.
key_quotes.context: Forward guidance on the near-term path, balancing inflation and jobs.

key_quotes.quote: And we actively watched the labor market to ensure there were no signs of stumbling.
key_quotes.context: Attention to labor-market downside risk as a driver of the easing decision.
